title: Olmos Park
name: Olmos Park
metaDescription: 1031 exchange coordination for the small McCullough Avenue commercial strip in Olmos Park, San Antonio's tightly held inner-loop enclave near Alamo Heights.

sections.html: Olmos Park is one of the smallest incorporated cities in the metro, a tightly held residential enclave sandwiched between Alamo Heights and the North Central San Antonio corridor, with almost all of its limited commercial activity concentrated along McCullough Avenue. For 1031 exchange buyers, that means a narrow but distinct opportunity set rather than a broad commercial submarket.

sections.heading: The McCullough Avenue Commercial Strip
sections.html:
Commercial property in Olmos Park is essentially limited to a short stretch of McCullough Avenue, where small retail buildings, professional offices, and a handful of restaurants serve the surrounding affluent residential neighborhoods. There is no significant industrial, multifamily, or big-box retail presence within the city's small footprint, so an exchange buyer targeting Olmos Park is specifically looking for this small-format retail and office product rather than a full range of asset classes.

These properties still qualify as like-kind real property held for investment or business use, so an investor exiting a different asset class elsewhere faces no classification issue identifying property here, though the small inventory pool means backup identifications elsewhere are worth having.

sections.heading: What Drives Demand in Olmos Park
sections.html: Proximity to Alamo Heights and the broader Broadway corridor pulls affluent, walkable-neighborhood demand into the McCullough Avenue strip, supporting rents that outperform many other small commercial strips in the metro relative to their size. Because the city is almost entirely built out and residential zoning dominates outside the strip, there is little prospect of new commercial supply, which tends to support pricing for the buildings that do exist.

sections.heading: Closing Coordination for an Olmos Park Property
sections.html:
Given how small and infrequently traded this strip is, confirming zoning and any use restrictions specific to the McCullough Avenue corridor is a necessary step before a property is placed on a written identification. Coordination for an Olmos Park file typically covers:

  • City-specific zoning and use-restriction confirmation for the McCullough Avenue strip
  • Written identification meeting the unambiguous-description standard under Treasury Regulation 1.1031(k)-1
  • Lease abstract and tenant estoppel review given typically long-held tenancies
  • Lender preflight against Olmos Park's thin comparable sales data
  • Exchange agreement and assignment documents routed to the qualified intermediary before funding

This tracks documentation and timeline; it is not a substitute for the investor's own legal or tax review.

sections.heading: Watchouts for Olmos Park Investors
sections.html: The extremely limited inventory means an investor relying on a single Olmos Park identification without a backup risks having no fallback if that specific deal falls through inside the 45-day window. Because so few properties trade, comparable sales data is thin, which can complicate lender appraisal, so having recent nearby Alamo Heights or North Central San Antonio comps ready to support valuation is worth doing in advance.

faqs.q: What kind of commercial property exists in Olmos Park?
faqs.a: Almost entirely small retail buildings, professional offices, and restaurants along McCullough Avenue. There is no meaningful industrial, multifamily, or big-box retail presence within the city's small footprint.

faqs.q: Why is Olmos Park inventory so limited?
faqs.a: The city is one of the smallest incorporated municipalities in the metro and is almost entirely built out with residential zoning outside the McCullough Avenue strip, leaving little room for new commercial supply.

faqs.q: Should I identify a backup property given how thin Olmos Park inventory is?
faqs.a: Most investors do, since the extremely limited commercial footprint means a single Olmos Park identification without a backup risks having no fallback if that specific deal falls through inside the 45-day window.

faqs.q: Is appraisal harder for Olmos Park properties given the thin comps?
faqs.a: It can be, since so few properties trade here. Having recent nearby comparable sales from Alamo Heights or North Central San Antonio ready to support valuation is worth preparing in advance.

faqs.q: Does Olmos Park's proximity to Alamo Heights affect its commercial rents?
faqs.a: Yes, the McCullough Avenue strip benefits from the same affluent, walkable-neighborhood demand that supports Alamo Heights' Broadway corridor, which helps rents outperform many comparably sized strips elsewhere in the metro.
